title: How Basel III Changes Where Capital Sits: Nonbank Subsidiaries as Equity Reservoirs - Liberty Street Economics
gist: When Basel III's binding capital minimums took effect for U.S. banks in January 2015, organizationally complex bank holding companies met tighter bank-level requirements not by raising costly external equity but by internally redeploying capital from equity-rich nonbank subsidiaries into their bank subsidiaries, leaving consolidated equity unchanged. Nonbank affiliates thus function as 'equity reservoirs,' and the size of that reservoir determines how strongly a BHC reallocates capital.

themes.theme: The regulatory-arbitrage mechanism behind bank-level capital divergence

insights.claim: After Basel III's binding capital minimums took effect for U.S. banks in January 2015, a bank holding company with a capital-short depository subsidiary could either raise costly fresh external equity or simply move existing capital from an equity-rich nonbank affiliate into the bank, and organizationally complex BHCs chose the latter.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: Headline bank-level capital ratios can strengthen without any new capital entering the consolidated firm, so investors should read post-2015 bank capital buildup as internal reshuffling rather than genuine de-risking of the overall organization.
insights.quote: The second route satisfies the regulator, avoids issuance costs, and leaves consolidated equity exactly where it was.
insights.locator: When Basel III's binding capital minimums

insights.claim: Internal reallocation from nonbank affiliates to bank subsidiaries satisfies the regulator and avoids equity-issuance costs while leaving consolidated equity, assets, lending, and leverage unchanged.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: Regulatory capital requirements applied at the bank level can be met without altering the true risk-bearing capacity of the consolidated firm, undermining the intended tightening effect of the rules.
insights.locator: When Basel III's binding capital minimums

insights.claim: The authors use the staggered timing of state interstate-banking deregulation during the 1970s, 1980s, and early 1990s as an instrument, because BHCs headquartered in earlier-deregulating states had more time to build complex multi-subsidiary organizations before national harmonization under the 1994 Riegle-Neal Act.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: The design isolates pre-existing organizational complexity from Basel III itself, strengthening the causal claim that complexity, not confounders, drove the capital-reallocation response.
insights.locator: To identify differences in organizational complexity

insights.claim: Banks in BHCs headquartered in states that deregulated one year earlier accumulate about three-quarters of a percentage point more excess capital after 2015, translating to roughly 5 percentage points more excess bank-level capital across the seven-year gap between early and late deregulators, while the consolidated-level estimate is essentially zero.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: The large bank-level effect paired with a null consolidated effect is the smoking gun that capital was moved internally rather than raised externally, a distinction that matters for judging true system resilience.

insights.claim: BHCs with greater deregulation exposure significantly reduced external equity issuance after 2015, meeting tighter requirements by drawing on internal resources rather than tapping outside investors.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: Complex banks' reduced reliance on equity markets to satisfy capital rules signals that tighter regulation may generate less new capital formation than headline ratios imply.
insights.quote: Rather than tap markets to meet tighter requirements, complex BHCs reduce their reliance on outside investors.
insights.locator: A large bank-level effect paired with a null consolidated effect

insights.claim: After 2015, parent equity investments in bank subsidiaries rose by 2.28 percentage points of total BHC equity per year of deregulation exposure while investments in nonbank subsidiaries fell by a nearly identical 2.64 percentage points, leaving total parent investments across all subsidiaries unchanged.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: The near-offsetting shifts confirm the parent relocates existing capital rather than injecting new capital, so bank-subsidiary strength is being purchased at the expense of nonbank-subsidiary capitalization.
insights.quote: The parent does not inject more capital into the organization; it changes where existing capital sits within it.
insights.locator: Step 1: The parent shifts equity toward banks

insights.claim: Following Basel III, dividends flowing from nonbank subsidiaries up to the parent increased sharply while dividends from bank subsidiaries showed little change, funding the equity injections into banks from the less-regulated side of the firm.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: Nonbank affiliates are being drained of cash to recapitalize banks, a flow that hollows out the nonbank side and is invisible at the consolidated level.
insights.quote: The parent effectively draws resources from the less-regulated side of the organization while leaving the regulated bank side largely untouched.
insights.locator: Step 2: The nonbanks supply the cash

insights.claim: After 2015, bank subsidiaries within BHCs that owned nonbank affiliates substantially increased retained earnings rather than distributing them upstream, whereas banks without access to nonbank affiliates showed no comparable change.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: Earnings retention is the third lever building bank capital, and the fact that only nonbank-connected banks do it links the buildup to the presence of an internal equity reservoir.

themes.theme: The equity reservoir and the equity multiplier

insights.claim: Nonbank subsidiaries function as 'equity reservoirs' because they hold a disproportionately large share of organizational equity relative to their assets, with the equity multiplier—the nonbank share of consolidated equity relative to its share of assets—measuring how much redeployable capital is available.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: The equity multiplier is a concrete metric investors and supervisors can compute to gauge a BHC's latent capacity to game bank-level capital rules through internal transfers.
insights.locator: The first post in this series introduced the idea

insights.claim: Each additional unit of the 2013 equity multiplier is associated with a 1.71 percentage point larger post-2015 increase in the share of consolidated equity invested in banks and a 1.12 percentage point larger decline in the share invested in nonbanks.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: The reallocation response scales with reservoir size, so BHCs with the largest pre-existing nonbank equity buffers are the ones whose bank capital ratios least reflect fresh capital.
insights.locator: Each additional unit of the 2013 equity multiplier

insights.claim: The availability of redeployable nonbank equity, not organizational complexity alone, determines how strongly a BHC responds to tighter capital requirements.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: Screening banks for reservoir capacity rather than mere complexity better predicts which firms can arbitrage capital rules internally.
insights.quote: The availability of redeployable nonbank equity, not organizational complexity alone, determines how a BHC responds to tighter capital requirements.
insights.locator: The chart below shows the same relationship

insights.claim: On the consolidated balance sheet, returns on assets and equity rose after Basel III while charge-offs fell with no shift toward riskier assets, indicating BHCs did not take more risk in aggregate but rather reallocated capital across differently regulated subsidiaries.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: Aggregate risk metrics look benign or improved, which can mask a redistribution of risk to specific subsidiaries that supervisors watching consolidated numbers would miss.
insights.quote: BHCs are not taking more risk in aggregate.
insights.locator: From the parent's perspective, redistributing funds

insights.claim: The same reallocation that strengthens banks weakens nonbank affiliates, shifts their activity toward riskier forms of lending, and creates a channel through which distress on the nonbank side can spill back onto the bank itself.
insights.speaker: Nicola Cetorelli and Shohini Kundu
insights.so_what: Investors should watch the drained nonbank affiliates as a hidden fragility point, since a bank strengthened on paper may still be exposed to contagion from its weakened, riskier nonbank siblings.
insights.quote: the same reallocation that strengthens banks weakens nonbank affiliates, shifts their activity toward riskier forms of lending, and creates a channel through which distress on the nonbank side of the organization can spill back onto the bank itself.
insights.locator: In the final post of this series
